messages.content:
Hey guys! I've been running some experiments for the challenge and I wanted to share some findings that y'all may find interesting. I wrote a program to apply the decision rules in my cheat sheet (ID EQT010003) deterministically and was able to show that they cover 85.72% of all the problems in the full 22 million problem matrix with 99.93% precision (one of the rules is more of a heuristic). On Hard3, this programmatic approach covers 18.8% of the problems and gets 66.75% accuracy (the rest fall into the DEFAULT bucket, which has about 50% accuracy), which is an upper bound for how well the LLM can do, assuming it makes no calculation errors.

V1 of my cheat sheet did poorly, with the worst performing rule M3 firing only 7 out of the 42 times (16.7%) it should have. I tested 5 different ways to present this rule and was able to get this up to 81% (a 9x improvement!) with no false fires through prompting alone. The most successful approach was to include a step-by-step algorithm, as well as a fully worked example. Testing this same approach on two other rules, however, I found that it helped on M2, but slightly hurt on M4. I believe this has to do with the rule type. On arithmetic/counting rules, a procedural step-by-step algorithm helps, but on pattern matching rules the model performs poorly across the board. My baseline model got 57.25% on Hard3, but with these prompt changes, I was able to get this up to 65.50%, close to the 66.75% upper bound!

This experiment was done on the GPT model. When testing on Llama I found that no matter how I prompted it, it had almost coin-flip accuracy on every rule. It was not able to reliably execute any of them for the hard problems. Curious as to if other people had a similar experience testing on this model?

Here is the full breakdown for the M3 experiment:

A. Control (2-line stub): 4/42 100% precision
B. Full Definition + 2 examples: 28/42 100% precision
C. Definition, no examples: 10/42 100% precision
D. Step-by-step algorithm + 1 example: 34/42 100% precision
E. Reference table + 1 example: 24/42 92% precision

Prompts (rest of the cheatsheet was kept the same):

A. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    Mirror of M2 using rightmost + right-depth.

B. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    Evaluation rule: ignore the left input. Walking from root through
    right children, every ◇ adds +1 mod 3 to the value. A term's value
    is (value of its rightmost leaf) + (right-depth) mod 3.
    → An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND right-depth(L) ≡ right-depth(R) (mod 3).

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ x)
      rightmost(L) = x;   rightmost(R) = x. ✓
      right-depth(L) = 0; right-depth(R) = 2. 0 ≢ 2 (mod 3).
      → Eq1 does NOT hold in right-cycle. (Not a witness; move on.)

      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      rightmost(L) = x;   rightmost(R) = x. ✓
      right-depth(L) = 0; right-depth(R) = 3. 0 ≡ 3 (mod 3). ✓
      → Eq1 holds. Now evaluate Eq2 the same way.

C. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same RIGHTMOST variable
    AND right-depth(L) ≡ right-depth(R) (mod 3).

D. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    To check whether an equation holds in right-cycle, perform these
    steps mechanically on each side:
      Step 1: Find the rightmost variable letter reading the side L→R.
      Step 2: Count the right-depth: starting from the root, walk into
              the right child repeatedly until you reach a leaf; count
              how many ◇ nodes you traversed.
      Step 3: Reduce right-depth modulo 3 (0, 1, or 2).
    The equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND their right-depths mod 3 are equal.

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      Step 1: rightmost of x = x. rightmost of y◇(z◇(w◇x)) = x. Same ✓
      Step 2: right-depth of x = 0. right-depth of y◇(z◇(w◇x)) = 3
              (root → right child z◇(w◇x); then → right child w◇x;
              then → right child x; 3 ◇ nodes walked).
      Step 3: 0 mod 3 = 0; 3 mod 3 = 0. Equal ✓
      → Eq1 holds in right-cycle.

E. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND their right-depths are in the SAME group below:
      Group A (right-depth mod 3 = 0): 0, 3, 6, 9
      Group B (right-depth mod 3 = 1): 1, 4, 7, 10
      Group C (right-depth mod 3 = 2): 2, 5, 8, 11

    Right-depth reference for common shapes:
      x                                 → right-depth 0 (Group A)
      y ◇ x                             → right-depth 1 (Group B)
      y ◇ (z ◇ x)                       → right-depth 2 (Group C)
      y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))                 → right-depth 3 (Group A)
      y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ (u ◇ x)))           → right-depth 4 (Group B)

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      LHS shape = x → right-depth 0 (Group A)
      RHS shape = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x)) → right-depth 3 (Group A)
      Rightmost matches (x = x). Both Group A → Eq1 HOLDS in right-cycle.
messages.subject: Cheat Sheet Harness Strategies

          (root → right child z◇(w◇x); then → right child w◇x;
          then → right child x; 3 ◇ nodes walked).
  Step 3: 0 mod 3 = 0; 3 mod 3 = 0. Equal ✓
  → Eq1 holds in right-cycle.

E. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}: a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
AND their right-depths are in the SAME group below:
Group A (right-depth mod 3 = 0): 0, 3, 6, 9
Group B (right-depth mod 3 = 1): 1, 4, 7, 10
Group C (right-depth mod 3 = 2): 2, 5, 8, 11

Right-depth reference for common shapes:
  x                                 → right-depth 0 (Group A)
  y ◇ x                             → right-depth 1 (Group B)
  y ◇ (z ◇ x)                       → right-depth 2 (Group C)
  y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))                 → right-depth 3 (Group A)
  y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ (u ◇ x)))           → right-depth 4 (Group B)

Worked example:
  Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
  LHS shape = x → right-depth 0 (Group A)
  RHS shape = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x)) → right-depth 3 (Group A)
  Rightmost matches (x = x). Both Group A → Eq1 HOLDS in right-cycle.

messages.content:
Arjun Garg said:

Hey guys! I've been running some experiments for the challenge and I wanted to share some findings that y'all may find interesting. I wrote a program to apply the decision rules in my cheat sheet (ID EQT010003) deterministically and was able to show that they cover 85.72% of all the problems in the full 22 million problem matrix with 99.93% precision (one of the rules is more of a heuristic). On Hard3, this programmatic approach covers 18.8% of the problems and gets 66.75% accuracy (the rest fall into the DEFAULT bucket, which has about 50% accuracy), which is an upper bound for how well the LLM can do, assuming it makes no calculation errors.

V1 of my cheat sheet did poorly, with the worst performing rule M3 firing only 7 out of the 42 times (16.7%) it should have. I tested 5 different ways to present this rule and was able to get this up to 81% (a 9x improvement!) with no false fires through prompting alone. The most successful approach was to include a step-by-step algorithm, as well as a fully worked example. Testing this same approach on two other rules, however, I found that it helped on M2, but slightly hurt on M4. I believe this has to do with the rule type. On arithmetic/counting rules, a procedural step-by-step algorithm helps, but on pattern matching rules the model performs poorly across the board. My baseline model got 57.25% on Hard3, but with these prompt changes, I was able to get this up to 65.50%, close to the 66.75% upper bound!

This experiment was done on the GPT model. When testing on Llama I found that no matter how I prompted it, it had almost coin-flip accuracy on every rule. It was not able to reliably execute any of them for the hard problems. Curious as to if other people had a similar experience testing on this model?

Here is the full breakdown for the M3 experiment:

A. Control (2-line stub): 4/42 100% precision
B. Full Definition + 2 examples: 28/42 100% precision
C. Definition, no examples: 10/42 100% precision
D. Step-by-step algorithm + 1 example: 34/42 100% precision
E. Reference table + 1 example: 24/42 92% precision

Prompts (rest of the cheatsheet was kept the same):

A. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    Mirror of M2 using rightmost + right-depth.

B. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    Evaluation rule: ignore the left input. Walking from root through
    right children, every ◇ adds +1 mod 3 to the value. A term's value
    is (value of its rightmost leaf) + (right-depth) mod 3.
    → An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND right-depth(L) ≡ right-depth(R) (mod 3).

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ x)
      rightmost(L) = x;   rightmost(R) = x. ✓
      right-depth(L) = 0; right-depth(R) = 2. 0 ≢ 2 (mod 3).
      → Eq1 does NOT hold in right-cycle. (Not a witness; move on.)

      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      rightmost(L) = x;   rightmost(R) = x. ✓
      right-depth(L) = 0; right-depth(R) = 3. 0 ≡ 3 (mod 3). ✓
      → Eq1 holds. Now evaluate Eq2 the same way.

C. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same RIGHTMOST variable
    AND right-depth(L) ≡ right-depth(R) (mod 3).

D. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    To check whether an equation holds in right-cycle, perform these
    steps mechanically on each side:
      Step 1: Find the rightmost variable letter reading the side L→R.
      Step 2: Count the right-depth: starting from the root, walk into
              the right child repeatedly until you reach a leaf; count
              how many ◇ nodes you traversed.
      Step 3: Reduce right-depth modulo 3 (0, 1, or 2).
    The equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND their right-depths mod 3 are equal.

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      Step 1: rightmost of x = x. rightmost of y◇(z◇(w◇x)) = x. Same ✓
      Step 2: right-depth of x = 0. right-depth of y◇(z◇(w◇x)) = 3
              (root → right child z◇(w◇x); then → right child w◇x;
              then → right child x; 3 ◇ nodes walked).
      Step 3: 0 mod 3 = 0; 3 mod 3 = 0. Equal ✓
      → Eq1 holds in right-cycle.

E. M3. RIGHT-CYCLE on {0,1,2}:  a ◇ b = (b + 1) mod 3.
    An equation holds iff BOTH sides have the same rightmost variable
    AND their right-depths are in the SAME group below:
      Group A (right-depth mod 3 = 0): 0, 3, 6, 9
      Group B (right-depth mod 3 = 1): 1, 4, 7, 10
      Group C (right-depth mod 3 = 2): 2, 5, 8, 11

    Right-depth reference for common shapes:
      x                                 → right-depth 0 (Group A)
      y ◇ x                             → right-depth 1 (Group B)
      y ◇ (z ◇ x)                       → right-depth 2 (Group C)
      y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))                 → right-depth 3 (Group A)
      y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ (u ◇ x)))           → right-depth 4 (Group B)

    Worked example:
      Eq1 = x = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x))
      LHS shape = x → right-depth 0 (Group A)
      RHS shape = y ◇ (z ◇ (w ◇ x)) → right-depth 3 (Group A)
      Rightmost matches (x = x). Both Group A → Eq1 HOLDS in right-cycle.

Would be very interesting to know how u mined the deterministic program to cover 85.72% of full 22 mil implications. This alone would be an interesting contribution to AI interpretability.
messages.subject: Cheat Sheet Harness Strategies

messages.content:
I basically wrote scripts to parse each equation, extract some structural features, and then encode rules based on those features in python to decide whether to output True or False. I also added the ability to test counterexamples and then, aside from some base rules that I came up with myself, used agents to try a bunch of rules and counterexamples. I treated Hard3 as a validation set to avoid overfitting. I had Claude help me make a repo if yall want to experiment on your own or test the upper bound for your own cheat sheets. I found it helpful to see a rule-by-rule breakdown of which rules the LLM could get right and how often to help me decide which ones to keep and which to remove.

https://github.com/arjun2garg/SAIR-Math-Distillation/tree/main
